items.myth: Euclidean distance is always the best default for k-NN.
items.reality: Euclidean is common on scaled continuous features, but Manhattan or cosine can outperform when data are sparse, axis-aligned, or embedding-direction-driven; the best metric is empirical.
items.example: On a sparse TF-IDF grid, k-NN with `metric='cosine'` often beats raw Euclidean because direction encodes topic overlap better than absolute counts.

items.myth: Minkowski distance with p=2 and cosine similarity always rank neighbors identically.
items.reality: After L2-normalization, cosine and Euclidean rankings often align, but on unnormalized vectors they diverge because cosine ignores vector length.
items.example: Embeddings (1, 2) and (10, 20) have cosine 1.0; their Euclidean distance is non-zero and they are not nearest neighbors of each other under L2 unless other points are farther.

items.myth: Changing the distance metric only affects speed, not model accuracy.
items.reality: The metric defines the decision boundary in k-NN and the cluster shapes in distance-based clustering; it can change accuracy, calibration and fairness materially.
items.example: On the same dataset, switching k-NN from p=2 to p=1 with k=5 can flip borderline query labels when features have different sparsity patterns.
